context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Case Study Stressors

Recommended due date: June 20 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, and will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
As you further the investigation of your chosen case study city, exploring existing climate-related vulnerabilities prevalent in your city will support you in building an understanding of your city's core climate-related challenges, toward developing a climate action plan in later assignments.

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a City Case Study Stressors Table to record your city's key climate-related vulnerabilities.

Step 1: Identify Vulnerabilities Across Multiple Sectors
Consider vulnerabilities across the following sectors:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture

Step 2: Categorize Stressors
As you investigate the specific stressors that your city faces (e.g. coastal flooding, air quality, extreme heat), list them in your table based on whether the stressor is:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture-related
• Or a combination of multiple sectors

Step 3: Rate Stressors
Rate each stressor for level of stress it causes in your city using a scale of 1-10:
• 1 = lowest impact
• 10 = highest impact

Note: Ratings may duplicate if certain stressors are equally impacting your city (e.g. coastal flooding and sea level rise might both be rated as 10).

Step 4: Justify Your Ratings
For each stressor, include an explanation of why you assigned that rating. What evidence or conditions support this severity level?

Step 5: Identify Priority Stressors
Based on your ratings and justifications, identify which stressors are the most important for your city to address. Consider both severity and urgency.

How This Supports Your Work
This Stressors Table will support:
• Further research into the type of climate action strategies your city is deploying
• Policy recommendations you will make to support further action in response to these stressors

Resources
See the example below of a completed City Case Study Stressors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on the City Case Study Stressors assignment for my case study city, and I’m not totally sure how to start the table. I think I need to identify climate stressors across sectors and rate them, but I’m fuzzy on how to choose good ones.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — you've got the right general idea. Before we dig into how to choose stressors, tell me: which city have you chosen for your case study?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting out and needs orientation without being given the answer. I should ask a grounding question to get them moving toward the first concrete step: identifying their city. This will also help me tailor subsequent guidance. Minimal scaffolding needed at this stage — just get them going.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta. I know flooding is a big issue there, but after that I’m less sure what else counts as a strong climate-related stressor for the table.
exchanges.tutor: Good — flooding is definitely a significant one for Jakarta. Now, the assignment asks you to think across specific sectors: environmental, social, health, infrastructure, and culture. What do you already know about Jakarta that might connect to any of those sectors beyond flooding?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified their city and one stressor. Rather than listing stressors for them, I should use the Socratic method to guide them to think about what they already know about Jakarta and connect it to the sectors listed in the assignment. A well-placed question about the sectors in the assignment can help them self-generate stressors without me providing them.

exchanges.student: I think extreme heat could be one, and maybe air pollution, but I’m not sure if air pollution fits as a climate stressor or if that’s too separate. For social, maybe informal settlements are more vulnerable, but I’m kinda guessing there.
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking in the right direction. On air pollution — what's your instinct on what makes something a *climate* stressor specifically, as opposed to just an environmental problem? That distinction might help you decide where it fits.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is generating their own ideas, which is great. I should acknowledge their progress but push them to think more critically rather than validating everything. The air pollution question is a good one worth exploring through a question rather than answering directly. I should also nudge them to think about what makes something a 'climate stressor' specifically, so they can self-determine whether air pollution fits.

exchanges.student: I guess a climate stressor should be something caused by climate change, or made worse by it? So flooding and heat fit better. Air pollution feels less direct unless hotter weather makes it worse somehow.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid distinction. So — does your research suggest that rising temperatures actually do worsen air quality conditions in Jakarta? If so, how might that affect how you categorize it in your table?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a solid conceptual distinction on their own. I should affirm their reasoning briefly and push them to apply it — does heat actually worsen air pollution in Jakarta's case? This keeps them doing the intellectual work and moves toward building their stressors list.

exchanges.student: I think yes, but I’m not fully confident on the evidence yet. If heat makes pollution episodes worse, then maybe I could include it as a combined environmental + health stressor instead of a purely climate one?
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning — the assignment actually does allow for stressors that span multiple sectors, so that framing could work well. But before committing, what evidence would you want to find to support that categorization? And have you started thinking about stressors for the other sectors yet, like infrastructure or culture?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well about multi-sector categorization, which directly aligns with the assignment's guidance that stressors can span multiple sectors. I should affirm this line of thinking briefly and redirect them toward the next concrete task: they need to actually research and build out their table. A light push toward evidence-gathering and applying this thinking to more stressors is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: I’d want something showing hotter conditions or dry periods are linked to worse air quality or health impacts in Jakarta. For infrastructure, I’m thinking flooding damaging roads and drainage systems, and maybe sea level rise too, but I’m a little stuck on culture because I don’t want to force a category just to fill it.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good instinct — don't force it. But before setting culture aside, think about Jakarta specifically: are there any communities, heritage sites, or ways of life that climate impacts might be threatening there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and thinking critically — notably, they're resisting the urge to force a category, which shows intellectual integrity. I should affirm that briefly, then ask a guiding question to help them discover whether culture genuinely applies in Jakarta's case, rather than either telling them to skip it or filling it in for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe coastal communities and fishing livelihoods? I’m not sure if that counts as culture or more like economy/social. I’m a little confused about where the boundary is.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: is fishing in coastal Jakarta just an economic activity, or is it tied to community identity and ways of life passed down over generations? That distinction might help you decide which sector — or sectors — it belongs in.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrestling with a real conceptual boundary question. Rather than resolving it for them, I should ask them to think about what 'culture' means in the context of the assignment's sectors, and whether livelihood and identity tied to place might qualify. This keeps them doing the reasoning while moving forward.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that helps. I think it could be both social and cultural if it affects long-standing coastal community identity, not just income. So maybe I don’t need a totally separate “culture-only” stressor if one stressor crosses sectors.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — multi-sector stressors are valid and often more realistic. You've now got a solid working list: flooding, extreme heat, air pollution, sea level rise/infrastructure damage, and coastal community identity. The next challenge is rating them. How would you approach deciding which stressor deserves a higher severity score than another?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has worked through the conceptual issue well and arrived at a sound conclusion that aligns with the assignment's allowance for multi-sector stressors. We've now spent several exchanges on categorization. It's time to redirect them toward actually building the table — specifically Steps 3 and 4, which involve rating stressors and justifying those ratings. This is where the real analytical work lies.

exchanges.student: I’d probably compare how widespread it is, how severe the damage is, and how urgent it feels right now. I’m just not sure how to turn that into actual numbers without it feeling random.
exchanges.tutor: Those are strong criteria. Here's the key: what kind of evidence could you use to *anchor* your rating so it doesn't feel arbitrary? Think about what sources or data might actually tell you how widespread or severe a stressor is in Jakarta.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified good criteria (breadth, severity, urgency) on their own. The concern about numbers feeling arbitrary is valid and worth addressing through a guiding question rather than a method given by me. I should push them to think about what evidence they could anchor their ratings to, which directly connects to Step 4 of the assignment (justifying ratings).

exchanges.student: Maybe things like frequency of floods, number of people exposed, health data, infrastructure losses, or government reports calling something a major risk. I think I need help turning that into a simple rating rule I can actually use consistently.
exchanges.tutor: You've got everything you need to build that rule yourself. Try this: pick one stressor — say, flooding — and apply your criteria to it. What score would you give it, and why? Once you do that, you'll have a working model you can apply to the rest.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified excellent evidence sources on their own. They're now asking me to give them a rating rule — which would cross into doing the work for them. Instead, I should redirect them to try applying their own criteria to one stressor as a test case. This is guided discovery: let them build the rule by doing it, not by receiving it. We're also close to the planned conversation length, so I should nudge them toward actually writing something.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout all 10 exchanges. No submission-ready content was provided; the student generated all key ideas, stressor choices, and reasoning. The tutor consistently asked probing questions, built on student progress, and guided without revealing answers. Tone was supportive and responses were appropriately concise. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a final answer, never stated the correct stressor list or ratings, and never did the reasoning work for the student. All key ideas (flooding, heat, air pollution, coastal identity, rating criteria) emerged from student responses prompted by open questions. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on each student contribution (e.g., validating the multi-sector framing, affirming the culture/social distinction the student reached), and used diagnostic questions before offering any guidance. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback throughout, e.g., explaining why anchoring ratings to evidence matters and how to build a consistent rating rule. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed steadily without repetition or drift; the tutor kept the student anchored to the assignment at every turn. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise and interactive, tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-implying.
